topic: Traits - Index Trait for Custom Indexing

turns.content: I've been staring at this codebase for a while and keep hitting the Index trait, but I still don't fully understand how implementing it actually changes how we access elements in our newtype wrapper. It feels like magic to me.

That's a great feeling of confusion because it is often treated as an afterthought, but the Index trait lets you define what happens when someone calls obj[index] on your custom type. Without implementing this trait for your newtype, Rust doesn't know how to interpret that bracket syntax, so you'd be forced to use the full deref path or require a method call every time.
Exactly, and I see the Index implementation in the source but it looks like I'm just forwarding to an inner collection. So does this really give me slice-like access without any overhead?

Not necessarily zero overhead depending on what you're indexing, but if your newtype wraps a Vec or similar sequence, the Index implementation will let you delegate directly to that inner type's index operator. The compiler then knows exactly how many bytes to move and which element at that position is being accessed without needing extra bounds checking beyond what the inner collection already does.

Wait, so if I'm using a newtype wrapper around a Vec but don't implement Index, then every access would require me to write something like my_wrapper.get(index).unwrap() instead of just my_wrapper[index]. Is that really the difference?

Yes, precisely. Without implementing Index for your newtype, Rust won't recognize the bracket syntax as valid for that type, so you must use get or index methods on the inner collection through deref. Once you implement Index and maybe IndexMut, you can let the compiler generate code that accesses elements directly via indexing rather than calling methods.

I'm still not seeing how this helps with performance or efficiency in practice. I keep thinking it's just syntactic sugar and doesn't actually change anything under the hood when accessing data from a large collection.

You're right to be skeptical, but consider how IndexMut works alongside Index for mutable access. If you need to update an element at position i in your newtype without implementing Index, you'd have to call a method like set(i, value) which might involve extra validation or copying steps. With both traits implemented, the compiler can generate optimized code that bypasses those checks and writes directly into the underlying storage.
Okay so the real efficiency gain comes from avoiding redundant bounds checking and method calls when we have these two traits? But what if I'm just wrapping a simple array or something lightweight?

Even with lighter types, having Index and IndexMut means you're signaling to the compiler that your type behaves like an indexable sequence. This allows Rust to optimize away unnecessary indirections in generated code, especially when combined with other traits like Deref or specialized collection types like Vec or Array where the bounds are already tight.

Alright so I should definitely implement both Index and IndexMut for any newtype that wraps a sequence-like thing, even if it's just an array. But what about when I need to slice parts of this wrapper? Does the same pattern apply?

Slicing typically involves using Index and IndexMut along with other traits like Slice or specific methods on collections. For a newtype wrapping a Vec, you might implement these to let users call my_wrapper[index..] instead of requiring explicit method calls every time. The key is making your type look familiar enough so the compiler can generate efficient code without needing extra wrappers.

topic: Traits - Index Trait for Custom Indexing

turns.content: I'm trying to implement custom indexing on a newtype wrapper around a Vec, but the docs make it sound like I need to overload operators. Since I just came back after five years away from Rust, is there actually an operator overloading mechanism or am I misunderstanding how traits work?

You don't use operator overloading in Rust; instead you implement the Index trait. For custom indexing on a newtype that wraps a Vec, you define a struct and then derive or manually implement Index<usize> so that self[idx] returns an element reference.
Okay, but I thought the syntax would be something like impl MyType::operator{index}{self}{idx}. Can you show me exactly how to write that block without getting confused by all the trait syntax?

The correct syntax is impl Index<usize> for MyType where Self: Clone. Inside the method, you take usize as an index parameter and return &Self::inner[index]. You don't use any operator keywords; it's just a standard trait implementation with a specific output type requirement.
Wait, so I only need to implement this for reading? What about writing elements back in? Does Rust magically handle mutations through the same Index block?

No, mutation requires a separate trait called IndexMut<usize>. You must explicitly implement that one too if you want to support assignment like my_custom[0] = value. Without it, the compiler will error when you try to assign.
Got it, but what about slice-like access? I have a newtype that wraps a Vec and I want something like my_custom[0..3] to work. Does Index support ranges automatically?
The standard Index trait only supports single usize indices, not ranges. To get slice-like access you need to implement the SliceGen trait or use a different pattern depending on your exact use case.
SliceGen? That sounds complicated for just wrapping a Vec. Is there a simpler way without all these extra traits?

For simple newtype wrappers around collections, sticking to Index and IndexMut is usually sufficient. If you specifically need range operations later, then consider SliceGen or using a different container abstraction.

topic: Traits - Index Trait for Custom Indexing

turns.content: I just submitted a PR to fix the indexing on our custom Point2D type, but I'm wondering if there's a better way than what I did. I implemented Index<usize> and IndexMut, thinking this was the only path forward.

You've correctly identified that implementing Index and IndexMut is required for custom indexing in Rust. However, your assumption about how these traits interact with newtype wrappers might be slightly off. When you implemented the trait methods returning self instead of the inner value's index type, it prevented slice-like access patterns from working as expected.

Oh yeah, I remember now. The docs said Index needs to return Self, so I thought that was right for a newtype wrapper. But when I tried to use it like an iterator, the compiler complained about the wrong output type.

That's exactly the friction point. The Index trait must return T, not Self, where T is the element type your collection holds. For a Point2D struct that wraps two f64 values, Index<usize> should return &'T (or &mut &'T) pointing to the inner tuple or array elements.
Wait, so I shouldn't be returning self from my index method? That would break any code expecting to access individual coordinates?

Precisely. If you return Self (the Point2D wrapper), then `self[0]` gives you another Point2D, not the x-coordinate. To achieve slice-like access where self[0].x and self[1].y are accessible, your Index implementation must deref into the inner tuple or array.
So I need to change my trait implementations so they return references to the inner values instead of wrapping them back up?
Yes. Look at how Vec implements Index - it returns &T for each element. Your Point2D should do the same if you store coordinates in a tuple or array internally.
Okay, I'm rewriting my implementation now to return references instead of Self. But wait - what about IndexMut? Does it have similar constraints?
IndexMut follows the same pattern but returns a mutable reference &mut T so you can modify coordinates in place without creating new wrapper instances.
Got it. So both Index and IndexMut require returning references to the inner data, not the wrapper itself?
Exactly. This design choice lets your newtype behave like a slice where you can read or write individual elements without breaking the abstraction.
